content:
• Cards in the 'scenario-specific' classification belong to a scenario's set of accompanying cards.
  • A scenario must include each such card in the exact quantity determined by the scenario. In most scenarios these are separated into the villain deck, main scheme deck, and encounter deck. Designated by the scenario's name printed at the bottom of the card.
See: Basic Power, Scheme (Enemy Activation).

The word 'scheme' denotes three different card types: main schemes, player side schemes, and side schemes. If a card ability places or removes threat from 'a scheme,' the resolving player chooses which scheme (main or side) that ability affects.

A scheme is a type of enemy activation. If an enemy is instructed to scheme:

  1. If a villain or villainous minion is scheming, give it one facedown boost card (skip for non-villainous minions).
  2. Resolve each boost card one at a time: flip faceup, resolve any 'Boost' ability, increase SCH by boost icons, discard the boost card, repeat.
  3. Place threat on the main scheme equal to the scheming enemy's modified SCH value.

• When instructed to search for a card, the player may look at each card in the searched area.
  • If the player finds a card satisfying the criteria, add it to the game area indicated by the search effect.
  • If multiple cards satisfy the criteria, the player chooses among those options.
  • Cards being searched are not considered to leave the searched area.
  • If any portion of a deck is searched, that entire deck is shuffled upon completion.
  • If instructed to 'search [their] collection,' the player looks through all their Marvel Champions cards outside the current game, becoming owner of that card until the game ends.
See: Referential Ability.

Some game steps or card abilities instruct players to set aside specific cards. Set-aside cards are out of play and have no interaction with the game until referenced by scenario instructions or a card ability.
The set icon is an indicator on a card of that card's product of origin, located in the card's collector information at the bottom of the card.
A card with the setup keyword begins the game in play. Cards with the setup keyword are put into play during the 'Put Setup Cards Into Play' step of setup.

'Setup' is a type of triggered ability resolved during setup. Setup abilities are mandatory.

Setup abilities on encounter cards are resolved during the 'Resolve Scenario Setup and When Revealed Abilities' step; on player cards, during the 'Resolve Character Setup Abilities' step.

Shuffling is the game function of randomizing the contents of a deck. If instructed to shuffle a deck, it must be randomized to the point where no player can ascertain the order.

Any time a deck is searched, that deck is shuffled after the search resolves.

Side scheme is an encounter card type representing additional obstacles and distractions confronting the heroes. If revealed, it enters play and is placed next to the main scheme deck in the villain's play area, with threat equal to its starting threat value.

It remains in play until there is no threat on it (defeated and discarded) or a card ability removes it from play; threat can be removed via basic thwart or card abilities.
If two or more effects with the same bold timing trigger would resolve simultaneously, the first player determines the order in which the effects resolve.
See: Modes of Play.
A special ability is a triggered ability indicated by the bold 'Special' timing trigger, resolved only through the explicit instruction of another card ability.

A character with the stalwart keyword cannot be stunned or confused.

  • Equivalent to:
    This character cannot have confused or stunned status cards.
  • If a character gains stalwart while it has a stunned/confused status card, that status card is removed.
See: Modes of Play.

The standard set is an encounter set added to most scenarios. It is not a modular encounter set and cannot be selected when a scenario requires choosing a modular set.

Cards in the 'Standard' classification have 'Standard' printed at the bottom of the card.

A star icon is used with a card's stat or boost field to indicate a mandatory ability in the text box corresponding to that field; in itself it has no effect, serving as a reminder to check the text box whenever that field is used (for ATK/SCH on an enemy or attachment, ATK/THW on an ally, ATK/THW/DEF on a hero, REC on an alter-ego, or an encounter card's boost field). If a game step/ability references a power with the value of star, that value is defined in that card's text (0 if undefined).

Status cards represent different states a character may find themselves in during the game.

  • When given a status card, take one of the specified type from the pool.
  • A character cannot have more than one status card of each type at a time (steady characters can have one additional confused and one additional stunned). Status card abilities have timing priority over all conflicting triggered abilities. Three types: Confused (cancels next scheme/thwart), Stunned (cancels next attack), Tough (prevents a character from taking damage).

A character with the steady keyword can have one additional stunned status card and one additional confused status card. It is not stunned unless it has two stunned status cards, and not confused unless it has two confused status cards.

After that character's attack/scheme/thwart is canceled by a status card effect, remove all status cards of the corresponding type from that character.

Stun is a status that cancels a character's next attack.

  • A stunned status card resolves like:
    Forced Interrupt: When this character would attack, remove each stunned status card from it instead.
  • A character is stunned if it has a stunned status card (steady characters need two).
  • If a character 'cannot be stunned,' stunned status cards cannot be placed on it.
  • A stunned identity/ally attempting to attack discards the stunned card instead (costs, including exhausting, still paid) — can attempt even with no valid target.
  • A stunned villain/minion that would attack discards the stunned status card instead.
  • The character is not considered to have attacked.
Some ally cards have a subtitle beneath the title, representing an alternate alias a character sometimes uses.

Support is a player card type representing locations, backline supporters or friends, and other behind-the-scenes elements at a hero's/alter-ego's disposal. Support cards enter play in the back row of a player's play area.

A support card is active while in play, remaining until a card ability causes it to leave play. Attacks, thwarts, defenses, action abilities, and triggered abilities that resolve from supports are not considered to be performed by that player's identity.

When an encounter card with the surge keyword is revealed, the player resolving the card deals themself a facedown encounter card from the top of the encounter deck.

  • Equivalent to:
    When Revealed: Deal yourself 1 facedown encounter card.
  • Complete the process of resolving the original card, and any triggered response abilities, before revealing the additional card.

Sustained damage refers to the difference between a character's maximum hit points and remaining hit points. For an identity/villain (hit point dial), subtract remaining hit points (per the dial) from maximum hit points.

Sustained damage on an ally/minion equals the total value of all damage tokens on the card.

• An instruction to 'swap' two components means to exchange the location of those two components.
  • A swap cannot be completed if there is not a component in both locations. Swapped cards maintain their orientation (ready/exhausted, faceup/facedown). Swapping a card in hand with the top card of a deck is not considered drawing.
  • When swapping a card in a play area with one in an out-of-play area: if they share a title, neither is considered to enter/leave play (tokens/attached/tucked/status cards transfer, state and hit point dial value are maintained); if they do not share a title, the in-play card is considered to leave play and the out-of-play card enters play (tokens etc. are not transferred, the card enters ready, and any hit point dial resets to the new card's printed value).
